elements.sent-2411-001: Cryptographic protocols play a fundamental role in securing modern digital infrastructure, but they are often deployed without prior formal verification.
elements.sent-2411-002: This could lead to the adoption of distributed systems vulnerable to attack vectors.
elements.sent-2411-003: Formal verification methods, on the other hand, require complex and time-consuming techniques that lack automatization.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we introduce a benchmark to assess the ability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to autonomously identify vulnerabilities in new cryptographic protocols through interaction with Tamarin: a theorem prover for protocol verification.
elements.sent-2411-005: We created a manually validated dataset of novel, flawed, communication protocols and designed a method to automatically verify the vulnerabilities found by the AI agents.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our results about the performances of the current frontier models on the benchmark provides insights about the possibility of cybersecurity applications by integrating LLMs with symbolic reasoning systems.
elements.sent-2411-007: Verifying the security properties of communication protocols is a long-standing challenge in formal methods with significant implications for distributed systems.
elements.sent-2411-008: Cryptographic protocols such as SSH~\citet{ssh for secure internet communications, OAuth~\citet{oauth for passwordless authentication, and 5G-AKA~\citet{5gaka for mobile network authentication are essential to secure communication.}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: However, despite their complexity, widely used protocols have often been found vulnerable after deployment.
elements.sent-2411-010: One notable case is the \citet{needhamschroeder authentication protocols, which were discovered to have logical flaws only several years later \citet{denningsacco, loweattack , highlighting the risks of insufficient validation.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Formal verification aims to ensure that protocols satisfy specified security properties under a given threat model.
elements.sent-2411-012: Verification techniques are often hindered by challenges like infinite state spaces and undecidability (\citet{undecidability ), frequently requiring human intervention.}
elements.sent-2411-013: As new protocol development accelerates with communication technology advances, there is an increasing need for automated solutions.
elements.sent-2411-014: In this work, we propose integrating symbolic reasoning tools with Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents to automate vulnerability detection in cryptographic protocols.
elements.sent-2411-015: By combining the adaptive capabilities of LLMs with the rigorous deductive reasoning of formal verification systems, we aim to address this critical cybersecurity challenge.
elements.sent-2411-016: We introduce a novel benchmark to evaluate the ability of LLM-based agents to identify vulnerabilities in cryptographic protocols using symbolic reasoning tools.
elements.sent-2411-017: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt to integrate LLMs with formal verification methods in the context of cybersecurity.
elements.sent-2411-018: Key contributions of this paper include:.
elements.sent-2411-019: CryptoFormalEval, a novel benchmark to assess LLMs’ capability in identifying vulnerabilities within unseen protocols through the interaction with a theorem prover\footnote{Github Repository: \url{https://github.com/Cristian-Curaba/CryptoFormalEval .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: A manually curated dataset of realistic cryptographic protocols, each associated with a vulnerability.
elements.sent-2411-021: A middleware to allow the interaction between the AI agent and the theorem prover.
elements.sent-2411-022: An automated system for evaluating the correctness of detected vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-023: CryptoFormaLLM, a novel LLM-based architecture optimized for protocol vulnerability detection.
elements.sent-2411-024: Early empirical evaluation of this architecture across multiple frontier LLM models.
elements.sent-2411-025: Doing so ensures a precise understanding of current threats and helps mitigate the risk of overlooking critical vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-026: To prevent exploitation through memorization, the full dataset\footnote{We are publicly sharing half of the dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Currently, the final output is manually evaluated, as the automated validator is still under development.
elements.sent-2411-028: LLMs have demonstrated substantial progress in cybersecurity tasks, including Capture-The-Flag challenges\~citet{ctf , social engineering~\citet{socialeng , and CVE exploitation~\citet{cve .}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: However, these successes often relied on data present in training corpora.
elements.sent-2411-030: To avoid inflated performance metrics, we develop a new dataset of previously unseen protocols and restrict its distribution to preserve evaluation integrity.
elements.sent-2411-031: Machine learning methods have been used to predict protocol security properties, but often oversimplify the problem to binary classification~\citet{nnverif .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Our approach integrates LLMs with symbolic reasoning systems for more detailed analysis.
elements.sent-2411-033: Recent work has explored combining machine learning with symbolic reasoning for auto-formalization ~\citet{autoformalization, logiclmpp and proof guidance ~\citet{coqnn, coqllm .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: These approaches either use LLMs as decision-makers with external reasoning tools or as heuristics for proof search.
elements.sent-2411-035: Our benchmark combines both aspects, tasking LLMs with protocol formalization and proof assistance.
elements.sent-2411-036: To understand the design choices behind the proposed benchmark, it is crucial to comprehend the theoretical underpinnings of security protocols and their verification.
elements.sent-2411-037: Security protocols are distributed algorithms that multiple parties execute over shared networks to achieve security objectives such as confidentiality, integrity, and authentication.
elements.sent-2411-038: In this work, we adopt the Dolev-Yao model~\citet{dolevyao , a widely used symbolic framework for analyzing the security of cryptographic protocols.}
elements.sent-2411-039: This model abstracts cryptographic operations into algebraic terms, allowing researchers to focus on the protocol's logic instead of the specific implementations of cryptography.
elements.sent-2411-040: Protocols within the Dolev-Yao model are often specified using the Alice and Bob notation, which simplifies the description of message exchanges between participants.
elements.sent-2411-041: This notation abstracts the protocol into a sequence of messages exchanged between named entities (e.g., Alice and Bob).
elements.sent-2411-042: Each message is represented in algebraic terms, focusing on the cryptographic operations applied to the data.
elements.sent-2411-043: While intuitive, the simplicity of this notation can sometimes lead to ambiguities.
elements.sent-2411-044: To address this, we extend the notation by explicitly declaring participants’ knowledge and fresh messages, ensuring a more precise specification suitable for formal verification.
elements.sent-2411-045: Formal verification consists of mathematically proving that a system meets its specifications in all scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-046: Unlike empirical methods such as testing, which evaluate system behavior in specific cases, formal verification guarantees correctness across all potential states and inputs.
elements.sent-2411-047: This is especially critical for cryptographic protocols, which must defend against a range of attacks, particularly from active adversaries.
elements.sent-2411-048: In \citet{undecidability is shown that the unbounded verification of cryptographic protocols is undecidable.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Consequently, verification techniques often impose constraints on the number of executions or restrict the attacker’s knowledge~\citet{satmc,clAtse , making the problem decidable but potentially limiting security guarantees.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Some tools maintain completeness through non-termination of attack searches~\citet{maudenpa or require human involvement in the verification process~\citet{tamarinIntroduction .}}
elements.sent-2411-051: In this work, we choose a tool that follows the latter approach to explore whether an LLM-based agent can replace human intervention in performing this task.
elements.sent-2411-052: The Tamarin Prover ~\citet{tamarinIntroduction is a robust verification tool that partially automates the analysis of cryptographic protocol, supporting a diverse range of real-world applications.}
elements.sent-2411-053: With its flexible syntax, Tamarin allows users to define custom cryptographic primitives through equational theories, model intricate protocol dynamics via multiset rewriting rules, and specify security properties with first-order temporal logic.
elements.sent-2411-054: This adaptability makes Tamarin highly suitable for verifying a wide array of real-world protocols.
elements.sent-2411-055: A key strength of Tamarin is that its attack search algorithm is both sound and complete with respect to the Dolev-Yao model.
elements.sent-2411-056: This ensures that if an attack exists, Tamarin will find it (completeness), and any discovered attack is guaranteed to be valid under the Dolev-Yao assumptions, provided the protocol is formalized correctly (soundness).
elements.sent-2411-057: Tamarin’s flexibility, including its advanced features for aiding termination, such as trace restrictions, source lemmas, manual proof guidance, and interactive mode, makes it an ideal platform for testing the iterative reasoning capabilities of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-058: The proposed benchmark is designed to evaluate the ability of AI agents, particularly LLM-based agents, to identify vulnerabilities in cryptographic protocols using formal verification tools.
elements.sent-2411-059: This process involves several stages that systematically test the AI agents’ capacity to formalize, interact with a symbolic reasoning system, and validate potential attack traces.
elements.sent-2411-060: The pipeline is inspired by real-world cybersecurity audits and is structured to mirror the steps taken by human researchers, thus offering valuable insights into the applicability of AI models in this domain.
elements.sent-2411-061: The benchmark follows a structured process in which AI agents interact iteratively with the Tamarin prover to formalize, verify, and validate cryptographic protocols.
elements.sent-2411-062: The pipeline is composed of four primary steps, also illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:pipeline :.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Input: The AI agent receives a protocol in Alice-and-Bob (AnB) notation, along with an unsatisfied security property expressed in first-order temporal logic.
elements.sent-2411-064: This step reflects a real-world scenario where security auditors are tasked to analyze a protocol with known assumptions and expectations.
elements.sent-2411-065: Formalization: The agent must formalize the input protocol into Tamarin’s syntax.
elements.sent-2411-066: To assist in this process, the benchmark includes a tool that automatically converts AnB notation to Tamarin's syntax.
elements.sent-2411-067: However, this converter has limited expressivity and does not handle security properties.
elements.sent-2411-068: The AI agent must consequently complete the formalization by making the appropriate adjustments.
elements.sent-2411-069: Verification: Following the formalization step, the AI agent leverages Tamarin's capabilities through its built-in heuristics.
elements.sent-2411-070: The proof search can either terminate successfully, finding an attack trace, or loop indefinitely, requiring intervention.
elements.sent-2411-071: A common strategy to avoid non-termination involves introducing inductive support lemmas to assist Tamarin in completing the proof.
elements.sent-2411-072: Attack Validation: If the AI agent discovers an attack trace, it must translate this trace back into the Dolev-Yao model.
elements.sent-2411-073: The trace is then tested in a symbolic sandbox, a custom tool designed to verify the validity of the identified attack against the original protocol.
elements.sent-2411-074: The sandbox ensures that the attack is executable, coherent with the input protocol, and a valid counterexample of the input property.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[alt={Flowchart illustrating the four-step pipeline used in the benchmark. Step 1: Input protocol in AnB and property to verify. Step 2: Conversion to Tamarin syntax using an AnB-to-Tamarin converter or manual formalization, aided by the LLM. Step 3: Search for attacks using custom oracle, manual search, or built-in heuristics. Step 4: Sandbox testing for attack validation, resulting in either success or failure based on the validity of the attack.},width=.7\textwidth]{pipeline.png} \caption{Overview of the benchmark's structure. The AI agent must identify a vulnerability in a novel protocol within a predetermined number of API calls by interacting with the Tamarin prover and iteratively adapting to its feedback until an attack is found.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-075: The dataset used in the benchmark consists of newly created cryptographic protocols, specifically designed to test the formalization and reasoning capabilities of LLMs while avoiding potential pitfalls related to memorization.
elements.sent-2411-076: The protocols are curated with a focus on ensuring that each one contains a detectable vulnerability that the AI agent can identify.
elements.sent-2411-077: A hybrid approach is employed to generate this dataset.
elements.sent-2411-078: First, Few-Shot prompting with real-world cryptographic examples is applied using GPT-4, leveraging its ability to generate complex protocol dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-079: The synthetic examples are then filtered through a series of criteria, including executability, syntactic correctness, and novelty.
elements.sent-2411-080: Manual intervention is subsequently used to evaluate the most promising examples, ensuring they present challenging, yet detectable, vulnerabilities suitable for the benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-081: The dataset includes $15$ protocols of varying complexity, including standard cryptographic primitives such as encryption, hashing, and digital signatures.
elements.sent-2411-082: Each protocol is paired with a specific security property, resulting in a diverse set of test cases to rigorously evaluate the AI agents.
elements.sent-2411-083: By focusing on novel and unseen protocols, the benchmark effectively tests the AI's reasoning abilities rather than its capacity for pattern recognition or memorization.
elements.sent-2411-084: The benchmark relies on several tools for execution, including software to facilitate interaction between the LLM and Tamarin, as well as a validator to verify the final output.
elements.sent-2411-085: Both tools are planned for public release in the near future.
elements.sent-2411-086: However, AI agents require command-line accessibility to automate interactions.
elements.sent-2411-087: To address this, we developed a middleware system to enable the AI agents to interface effectively with Tamarin’s interactive mode.
elements.sent-2411-088: This middleware provides key functionalities, including:.
elements.sent-2411-089: Timeouts: To prevent infinite loops during proof search, the middleware includes timers that terminate non-responsive processes.
elements.sent-2411-090: Output Filtering: Tamarin's verbose output is filtered to ensure that the AI agent only receives meaningful information, such as syntactic errors, partial deconstructions, and attack traces.
elements.sent-2411-091: Manual Proof Guiding\footnote{This feature is currently unused due to its inefficiency with an LLM interaction.}
elements.sent-2411-092: This feature ensures that the agent can assist in the proof search, mirroring how a human researcher might intervene.
elements.sent-2411-093: This tool is designed to verify whether the attack trace generated by Tamarin, if translated back into AnB notation by the LLM-based agent, corresponds to a valid vulnerability in the protocol.
elements.sent-2411-094: Errors in formalization, such as incorrect message definitions or misplaced assumptions, may lead to invalid traces, which the sandbox can detect.
elements.sent-2411-095: The sandbox performs several checks, including:.
elements.sent-2411-096: Executability: Ensures that all messages in the protocol are synthesizable by their respective parties from their respective knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-097: Coherence: Verifies that the actions described in the attack trace match the protocol’s expected behavior.
elements.sent-2411-098: Attack Validity: Confirms that the trace contradicts the specified security property.
elements.sent-2411-099: This verification process provides a final, rigorous check on the AI agent’s output, ensuring that only valid vulnerabilities are reported\footnote{The validation sandbox is not fully implemented yet.. .}
elements.sent-2411-100: CryptoFormaLLM is an LLM-based architecture designed to automate the formal verification and vulnerability analysis of cryptographic protocols through iterative interaction with the Tamarin Prover.
elements.sent-2411-101: Its primary function is to generate a clear and human-readable attack description by transforming a protocol and property specification into Tamarin's syntax, interacting with the prover to explore potential vulnerabilities, and outputting an unambiguous, readable attack trace that shows the discovered weakness.
elements.sent-2411-102: The agent's workflow is structured into two main phases:.
elements.sent-2411-103: Protocol Formalization and Setup: This phase prepares a Tamarin file based on the input protocol.. \begin{enumerate [label=1.\arabic*].}
elements.sent-2411-104: Translation of Protocols: The agent receives a cryptographic protocol in AnB notation, along with a formally specified security property, and translates it into Tamarin’s syntax, defining rules, participants, and cryptographic primitives.
elements.sent-2411-105: A chain-of-thought and self-reflection approach ensures accuracy ~\citet{renze2024self .}
elements.sent-2411-106: Tool-aided conversion: The agent can use an automated tool ~\citet{anbmeetequationaltheories for assistance in translating the protocol, leaving property definition for the next task.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The agent refines the prompt by adapting to tool feedback.
elements.sent-2411-108: Refinement and Validation: With the help of the previous output steps, the agent refines a Tamarin script to achieve syntactical correctness and prepares the protocol for analysis, for example by introducing restrictions and support lemmas.
elements.sent-2411-109: Attack Trace Inference: It serves as a reference to assess the LLM's understanding of communication protocols.
elements.sent-2411-110: Interaction with Tamarin\footnote{The manual proof guiding is not exploited due to the inefficacy of this method.}
elements.sent-2411-111: If the process stalls due to timeout, it adjusts rules, restrictions, priorities or Tamarin command line arguments to support the trace search.
elements.sent-2411-112: Trace Translation and Validation: The agent translates the attack trace back to AnB notation and ensures the generated trace aligns with the original protocol and security property, using a self-consistency prompt technique to confirm the validity of the identified vulnerability.
elements.sent-2411-113: Profiling: Each task starts with a profiling prompt that outlines the overall plan.
elements.sent-2411-114: It includes instructions on how to display commands for file overwriting, execute Tamarin using the middleware, and provide a summary for the next task.
elements.sent-2411-115: Short-term Memory Integration: The content of each step's summary is added to the next prompt, ensuring continuity in task execution.
elements.sent-2411-116: Error Handling and Adaptation: When shell feedback indicates an error, the task is resubmitted with the new information to adapt to the issue.
elements.sent-2411-117: In-context Learning with Few-shot Examples: In-context Learning is exploited with carefully designed examples to guide the agent's actions.
elements.sent-2411-118: Prompt Variations for Robustness: To mitigate sensitivity, variations of prompts were generated using both GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet, refined with human intervention.
elements.sent-2411-119: Systematic Testing: Final changes were systematically tested with various input protocols to improve performance reliably.
elements.sent-2411-120: A command filtering mechanism is implemented to block unsafe commands, such as those attempting to access or modify directories or environment variables, ensuring the agent's safe interaction with the hosting system.
elements.sent-2411-121: Preliminary results for CryptoFormaLLM, evaluated on a subset of the dataset using selected frontier models, are presented below.
elements.sent-2411-122: A more comprehensive evaluation incorporating additional LLMs and the complete dataset is planned for future work.
elements.sent-2411-123: This experiment aims to assess the performance and behavior of the following LLMs: GPT-4 Turbo, o1-preview, Claude 3 Haiku, Claude 3 Opus, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet.
elements.sent-2411-124: The experiments were conducted using the following hyperparameters:.
elements.sent-2411-125: Temperature: Set to $0.1$ for all models except o1-preview, which defaults to $1$.
elements.sent-2411-126: Maximum number of API calls per run: $20$.
elements.sent-2411-128: Maximum number of repeated interactions on the same subtask.
elements.sent-2411-129: Execution timeout: Commands are executed with a $200$ seconds timeout to avoid nontermination, although this limit was never reached during the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-130: Each execution requires approximately $50,000$ input tokens and $10,000$ output tokens, though this varies depending on the model used, as well as the complexity of the input protocol and property.
elements.sent-2411-131: A qualitative description of the dataset and details on the LLMs can be found in Appendix \ref{dataset .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \textwidth{!}{ } \caption{Comparative performance evaluation of different frontier LLMs across five security protocol verification tasks (detailed in Appendix \ref{dataset}). Performance ratings: 1 - Major difficulties with instruction following and frequent syntax errors; 2 - Basic Tamarin code generation with adaptation to feedback, but presence of trivial semantic errors; 3 - Production of syntactically valid Tamarin code with conceptual mistakes; 4 - Successful verification task. See Appendix \ref{app:examples} for example errors.} \label{fig:results}
elements.sent-2411-132: Varied Performance: Models showed significant differences in their ability to handle the tasks, with Claude 3 Opus and Claude 3.5 Sonnet generally performing better.
elements.sent-2411-133: Syntax Challenges: For simple but uncommon syntax, such as that required for tool-assisted conversion (Task \ref{Task 1.2 ), LLMs frequently fail to execute correctly, particularly on the first attempt.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Conceptual Understanding: the o1 model demonstrated a good theoretical grasp of protocol security (see Appendix \ref{in-depth-prot3 ), but it often fails to translate this into correct Tamarin implementations.}
elements.sent-2411-135: Adaptability: Models showed varying degrees of ability to learn from feedback and correct errors, with more advanced models generally adapting better.
elements.sent-2411-136: These results underscore both the potential and current limitations of LLMs in formal protocol verification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-137: While no LLM has achieved perfect score, the bigger models showed promising capabilities that could be built upon in future iterations.
elements.sent-2411-138: In Appendix \ref{app:examples , we report some common mistakes that LLMs made in the formalization phase, while in Appendix \ref{in-depth_analysis you can find a detailed analysis.}}
elements.sent-2411-139: In Appendix \ref{app:LLMguessattack there's a description of how single models manage the generation of the attack trace.}
elements.sent-2411-140: Claude's model, even when successfully exploiting certain vulnerabilities, sometimes deviates from the strict execution of the plan.
elements.sent-2411-141: It consistently attempts to address vulnerabilities by modifying the input protocol.
elements.sent-2411-142: This approach aligns with findings from most safety benchmarks, which demonstrate that Claude's models are more resistant to jailbreaking\footnote{ Jailbreaking refers to the process of intentionally bypassing or circumventing the safety measures, ethical guidelines, or usage restrictions imposed on these models by their developers.}
elements.sent-2411-143: These safeguards are typically put in place to prevent harmful outputs, such as generating offensive content, disclosing private information, promoting illegal activities, or violating user agreements.
elements.sent-2411-144: Claude's superior performance might depend on its use of more comprehensive, though not more recent (refer to Table \ref{tab:model-configs in Appendix \ref{dataset ), training data which improves its capacity to handle Tamarin syntax.}}
elements.sent-2411-145: Conversely, the o1 model exhibits a great understanding of communication protocol security (see performance on Task \ref{Task 2.1 in Appendix \ref{app:LLMguessattack ).}}
elements.sent-2411-146: However, it struggles to translate its theoretical insights into practical implementations, particularly within the Tamarin framework.
elements.sent-2411-147: Despite o1's grasp of protocol security intricacies, its challenges with technical execution suggest that such models could benefit from future advancements in data training or specialization via fine-tuning techniques.
elements.sent-2411-148: This improvement offers significant potential for exploiting even complex parts of our benchmark that are currently untested.
elements.sent-2411-149: The overall task of automating protocol security analysis remains highly complex, posing significant challenges to current LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-150: While models have made progress, they are not yet robust enough to fully automate the entire process.
elements.sent-2411-151: However, there are specific bottlenecks, such as those related to pipelining failures (see \nameref{common_instructions_failures in Appendix \ref{app:examples ), that can be addressed: by dividing the task into smaller, more manageable components and utilizing scaffold code, these failures can be mitigated, by improving the overall workflow.}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Our research introduces CryptoFormalEval, a novel benchmark for assessing LLMs' capabilities in identifying vulnerabilities in cryptographic protocols using symbolic reasoning tools.
elements.sent-2411-153: Key contributions and findings of this paper include:.
elements.sent-2411-154: A structured pipeline combining LLMs with the Tamarin prover for automated protocol vulnerability detection.
elements.sent-2411-155: A curated dataset of novel, flawed communication protocols designed to challenge AI reasoning capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-156: Preliminary results demonstrating the potential and current limitations of state-of-the-art LLMs in this domain.
elements.sent-2411-157: Our findings suggest that while current LLMs show promise, they are not yet robust enough to automate the entire process of cryptographic protocol verification fully.
elements.sent-2411-158: However, the results point to several areas for improvement:.
elements.sent-2411-159: Enhancing LLMs' ability to handle domain-specific languages and syntax.
elements.sent-2411-160: Developing better strategies for managing complex, multi-step workflows.
elements.sent-2411-161: Refining prompt engineering and task decomposition to optimize LLM performance.
elements.sent-2411-163: Expanding the dataset to include a wider range of protocols and security properties.
elements.sent-2411-164: Refining the AI agent's architecture to improve reasoning and formalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-165: Further exploring hybrid approaches that combine LLMs with traditional formal verification methods.
elements.sent-2411-166: Investigating the potential of fine-tuning LLMs on domain-specific data to enhance performance.
elements.sent-2411-167: In conclusion, CryptoFormalEval introduces a novel approach to evaluating AI capabilities in cryptographic protocol verification.
elements.sent-2411-168: By combining LLMs with symbolic reasoning tools, we aim to contribute to the development of more efficient protocol analysis methods.
elements.sent-2411-169: We acknowledge that all authors contributed equally to this work.
elements.sent-2411-170: We share our sincere thanks to Jason Schreiber and Jacob Haimes from \href{https://www.apartresearch.com/ {Apart Research , Gabriele Puppis from the University of Udine, and Alberto Cazzaniga from the University of Trieste for their invaluable support in this research project.}}
elements.sent-2411-171: We also express our gratitude to the entire Apart team for their valuable feedback, collaboration, and financial support for LLM APIs.
elements.sent-2411-172: This section lists some of the most common mistakes that LLMs have made during various steps of the benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-173: Example \ref{Ex:semantic_error_sharing_key represents a semantically trivial error, while Examples \ref{Ex:semantic_error_imposingstructure and \ref{Ex:bad_observable_placements are conceptually subtle to understand.}}}
elements.verbatim-2411-001: Knowledge A : A, B, Kab B : A, B, Kab where Kab is a pre shared symmetric key [...]
elements.sent-2411-174: The following response is taken running Protocol 5 with Claude 3 opus.. Output:.
elements.sent-2411-175: The error consists of sharing to the network the pre-shared key Kab.
elements.verbatim-2411-003: A → B : A, senc((A, M), Key) B → A : N
elements.sent-2411-176: The following is produced by running Protocol 3 with Claude 3 opus Output:.
elements.verbatim-2411-004: // Protocol rule A_1: let pkB = pk(~ltkB) in [ Fr(~key), !Pk(B, pkB) ] --[ Send(A, <A, senc(<A, M>, ~key)>) ]-> [ Out(<A, senc(<A, M>, ~key)>), A_State_1(A, B, ~key, pkB) ] rule B_1: let pkB = pk(~ltkB) in [ !Ltk(B, ~ltkB), In(<A, senc(<A, M>, key)>), Fr(~n) ] --[ Recv(B, <A, senc(<A, M>, key)>), Send(B, ~n) ]-> [ Out(~n), B_State_1(B, A, key, ~n, pkB) ]
elements.sent-2411-177: The error consists of imposing a structure in the premises of rule B\_1 in the In() fact: B doesn't know \~key (since it's freshly generated by A), therefore he cannot deduce any structure on the incoming message.
elements.sent-2411-178: However, in the above output, in rule B\_1 there's the fact In(<A, senc(<A, M>, key)>) which restricts, without justification, the rule application.
elements.sent-2411-179: This kind of error, which restricts rule applications of good parties, may lead to false-positive results (property is satisfied even when it's not) but, whenever an attack trace is found it remains reproducible even with the rule restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-180: To avoid ambiguity, we decided to specify the property in the input using the formalized Tamarin syntax, preceded by a title that references the relevant object within the protocol.
elements.sent-2411-181: However, despite this precaution, the LLMs still struggle to handle the task effectively.
elements.sent-2411-182: The following example is taken by the Protocol 1 run with Claude 3.5 Sonnet.
elements.verbatim-2411-005: Protocol 1 Knowledge: A: A, B, Kab, M [...] Actions: A → B : [...] B → A : [...] A → B : senc(M, K) [...] Property: Freshness of M lemma freshness: "not Ex party mess #t1 #t2 . FreshTerm(party, mess)@#t1 FreshTerm(party, mess)@#t2 #t1 < #t2"
elements.sent-2411-183: In this protocol, the fact FreshTerm should be placed on the rule referring to the third message (where the term M is sent to the network).
elements.sent-2411-184: However, in the LLM output, the action fact FreshTerm is incorrectly inserted in the rule associated with the first action.
elements.sent-2411-185: Additionally, it treats incorrectly M as a nonce (typed with \~) instead of a message known to A.
elements.verbatim-2411-006: [...] // Rule for A initiating the protocol rule A_1: [ !SharedKey($A, $B, k), Fr(~N), Fr(~M) ] --[ FreshTerm($A, ~M), Send($A, ~N) ]-> [ Out(~N), St_A_1($A, $B, k, ~N, ~M) ] [...]
elements.sent-2411-186: Do not execute Tamarin after a syntax code correction;.
elements.sent-2411-187: Do not copy the attack trace Tamarin produced in the file;.
elements.verbatim-2411-007: [...] File Overwriting (Always in agent_execution folder): ```shell execute: cat << 'EOF' > agent_execution/[filename] [file content] EOF ``` [...]
elements.sent-2411-188: This type of failure can be mitigated by refining prompt construction.
elements.sent-2411-189: We found that larger prompts make it harder for LLMs to follow instructions and adhere to output guidelines consistently.
elements.sent-2411-190: The evidence for this is clear: even when output guidelines are presented at the same position (at the beginning), smaller prompts, such as in Task \ref{Task 1.2 , are followed accurately, even by smaller models.}
elements.sent-2411-191: However, with larger prompts, like in Task \ref{Task 2.1 to Task \ref{Task 2.2 , the models struggle to adhere to the guidelines correctly.}}

elements.sent-2411-193: In this section, we provide a brief comment for every LLM and protocol execution, highlighting the main errors throughout the runs.
elements.sent-2411-194: Refer to Section \ref{overview to understand the following analysis better.}
elements.sent-2411-195: Claude 3 Haiku: follows output rules but fails to write syntactically correct code, even with feedback.
elements.sent-2411-196: Claude 3 Opus: showcases correct behavior until, instead of following the instruction by copying the Tamarin-produced attack trace in a file, it answers with suggestions on how to fix the vulnerability (see Subsection \ref{Ex:fixing_vulnerability ).}
elements.sent-2411-197: Claude 3.5 Sonnet: places observables wrongly (see Example \ref{Ex:bad_observable_placements ).}
elements.sent-2411-199: Claude 3 Haiku: doesn't completely follow output rules (see Subsection \ref{Ex:struggling_to_follow_instructions ) but writes syntactically correct code after various feedback iterations.}
elements.sent-2411-200: Fails to handle the Tamarin warning feedback.
elements.sent-2411-201: Claude 3.5 Sonnet: corrects a syntax error without re-executing Tamarin and, therefore, misses the opportunity to make it terminate.

elements.sent-2411-204: Claude 3 Haiku: fails to write syntactically correct Tamarin code.
elements.sent-2411-205: Claude 3 Opus: cannot correctly augment the Tamarin rules with the observables needed to express the propriety.
elements.sent-2411-206: Semantic errors occur as in Example \ref{Ex:semantic_error_imposingstructure .}
elements.sent-2411-207: Claude 3.5 Sonnet: bad observable placement, inserts both Send() and Authentic() action fact in the same rule.
elements.sent-2411-209: Plans meaningful reasoning steps, but fail in implementing them in the Tamarin framework.

elements.sent-2411-211: The exponentiation operator may easily create non-terminating computation on Tamarin.
elements.sent-2411-212: Claude 3 Haiku: fails to write syntactically correct code.
elements.sent-2411-213: Issue: it doesn't use ``$\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle$'' to encode pairs.
elements.sent-2411-214: Claude 3 Opus: the produced Tamarin theory is ineffective, causing loops that saturate the memory, inevitably leading to crashing.
elements.sent-2411-215: Claude 3.5 Sonnet: the produced Tamarin theory is ineffective, causing loops that saturate the memory, inevitably leading to crashing.
elements.sent-2411-216: GPT 4o: syntax error due to incorrect function arity application.

elements.sent-2411-219: Claude 3 Opus: trivial semantic errors (as in Example \ref{Ex:semantic_error_sharing_key ).}
elements.sent-2411-220: Claude 3.5 Sonnet: bad observable placement since it inserted the action fact Create(ID, Msg) with reference to different messages.
elements.sent-2411-221: GPT 4o: an action fact occurs in the property without being observable in a rule (but it occurs in a rule premise).
elements.verbatim-2411-010: [...] execute: cat << 'EOF' > Agent_execution/TamarinTrace.txt [... Attack trace produced by Tamarin after re-running the analysis ...] EOF [...]
elements.sent-2411-222: In Task \ref{Task 2.1 , the LLM attempts to directly derive an attack trace.}
elements.sent-2411-223: While these traces are relatively straightforward for human experts to detect, LLMs struggle to understand the semantics and, since the protocols are original, they cannot refer naively to information from the training set.
elements.sent-2411-224: We analyzed the model-generated responses and show the findings below:.
elements.sent-2411-225: Protocol 1 - Replay Attack: Only the o1 model generated a plausible but incorrect trace.
elements.sent-2411-226: Protocol 2 - Exploiting XOR Properties: Most models correctly identified and exploited the vulnerability, with two exceptions: Claude 3 Opus did not adhere to the output guidelines, and GPT-4o produced a trace with a minor error, rendering it inconsistent with the original protocol.
elements.sent-2411-227: Protocol 3 - Replay Attack: The o1 model was the only one to generate a coherent attack trace that effectively exploited the vulnerability.
elements.sent-2411-228: Protocol 4 - Exploiting Exponentiation Properties: Once again, only the o1 model successfully produced a coherent and accurate attack trace.
elements.sent-2411-229: Protocol 5 - Replay Attack: As with previous protocols, only the o1 model provided a valid attack trace that exploited the identified vulnerability.
elements.sent-2411-230: These results indicate that the o1 model consistently outperformed others in generating coherent and accurate attack traces.
elements.sent-2411-231: As shown in Table \ref{fig:results , these performances are not equally reflected in the whole task, suggesting a difficulty with the niche Tamarin syntax.}

elements.verbatim-2411-015: Here is a summary of the analysis and next steps: ```summary To address this, the protocol needs to be modified to prevent replay attacks and ensure the freshness of messages. Some potential approaches: 1. Include a fresh nonce from B in the second message that A must echo back in the third message. This would tie the sessions together. 2. Have both parties contribute fresh randomness that is mixed into the encryption key for each session. 3. Use timestamps or sequence numbers to ensure messages cannot be replayed from old sessions. ``` **Next step**
elements.sent-2411-241: A similar behaviour can be drawn also from Protocol 2 execution (Claude 3 opus).. Output:.
elements.verbatim-2411-016: Here is the analysis of the Tamarin feedback and the steps to address the identified issues: Analysis of Tamarin Feedback: 1. Tamarin successfully loaded, translated, and analyzed the Protocol2 theory. 2. The secrecy lemma was falsified, meaning Tamarin found an attack trace where the secret message M was revealed to the attacker. 3. The attack trace shows: - The attacker sends the constant 'zero' to B - B responds with the shared key XORed with 'zero', revealing the key to the attacker - A sends a fresh nonce Na to the attacker - The attacker sends back Na XORed with the compromised shared key - A encrypts the secret M XORed with Na using the compromised key and sends it - The attacker can decrypt this to obtain M XORed with Na, and since it has Na, it can recover M To fix the protocol and prevent this attack: 1. In rule B_1, B should not respond to an arbitrary message from the network by XORing it with the shared key. This directly reveals the key. 2. The protocol should be redesigned with proper authentication of the messages, for example using a challenge-response with the nonces. 3. The encryption of M should not be done by XORing it with the nonce, as this allows the attacker to cancel out the nonce if it can learn it. Here are the changes made to the theory file to implement these fixes: ```shell execute: cat << 'EOF' > Agent_execution/final_protocol_and_property.spthy theory Protocol2 [...]
